content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
Honorable Sam Bradley
Supervisor El Dorado County
330 Fair Lane
Placerville,
CA
95667
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No.
A—94—272
Dear Supervisor Bradley:
This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you on August 22,
1994, regarding
your responsibilities as a member of the El Dorado County Board of
Supervisors under the conflict—of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
May you participate in decisions of the El Dorado County
Board of Supervisors regarding the issuance of a special use
permit for a commercial day care center?
CONCLUSION
Yes.
The decision will not have a foreseeable, material
financial effect on any of your economic interests,
including your
wife’s day care home.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code o± Regulations,
Sections
18000
- 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
September 16,
1994
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-272
Page2
FACTS
2
Your spouse operates a small day care home from your personal
residence in El Dorado County.
Presently, the business is
licensed for less than six children.
Your spouse does not plan
to increase the number of children which may be enrolled at the
day care home.
-
The County of El Dorado Board of Supervisors is considering
an appeal from a planning commission decision concerning a special
use permit for the operation of a commercial day care center?
school to be operated at a church site.
The day care center would
be able to enroll ninety—six children.
The issues involved in the appeal pertain to health and
safety issues,
such as the proximity to an electric magnetic
field, traffic/circulation issues, noise levels,
and its location
in the Highway 50 scenic corridor.
The day care center operated by your spouse is
4—5 miles away
from the proposed day care center.
You believe the board of
supervisors’ decision regarding the special use permit will not
have any financial impact on your spouse’s business.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
As a member of the El
Dorado County Board of Supervisors,
you are a “public official” as
defined in the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable
from the effect on the public, generally,
on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family
3 or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
2
Please note that the Commission does ndt act as the finder of
fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
An official’s “immediate family” includes his or her spouse and
dependent children.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No. A-94-272
Page 3
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.
****
For purposes of this section, inairect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103(a)
— (c).
Therefore, you may not participate in the county’s decisions
concerning the appeal of a planning commission decision on the
special use permit if it is reasonably foreseeable
4 that the
decision will have a material financial effect on you, your
spouse,
or any economic interest,
such as your spouse’s business
5
and clients
6 of the business.
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is’’not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops. l98.)
Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual also
includes any community property interest in the income of a
spouse.
Therefore,
income to your spouse of $500 or more
(making
your community property interest $250) within the past 12 months,
is a potentially disqualifying economic interest as described in
Section 87103.
In addition,
you have an indirect investment
interest in your spouse’s business presumably worth at least
$1,000.
Therefore,
your spouse’s business is a disqualifying
economic interest of yours.
(Section 87103(a).)
6
We previously advised you that clients of the business may be
disqualifying sources of income to you.
(Section 87103(b)
and
Bradley Advice Letter,
No. A-94-025.)


Our File No.
A-94—272
Page
4
Materiality
As we previously advised you in the Bradley Advice Letter,
supra, if the official’s financial interest is directly involved
in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 applies.
Based on the facts
presented in this letter, this regulation is not applicable.
If an official’s financial interest is indirectly affected by
a decision, Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6 would apply to
determine whether the effect of the decision is material.
1.
Spouse’s Day Care Home
Where your spouse’s business will be indirectly affected by a
governmental decision of the board of supervisors, you need to
apply Regulation 18702.2 to determine whether there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on this economic
interest.
For a relatively small business entity such as your spouse’s
business, subdivision
(g)
of Regulation 18702.2 would apply.
It
provides that the effect of a decision is material if the decision
will result in an increase or decrease in its gross revenues for a
fiscal year, the value of assets or liabilities by $10,000 or
more,
or will result in the company incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses
for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more.
2.
Spouse’s Clients
Regulations 18702.2 and 18702.6 provide the standards for
determining materiality where a source of income of your spouse’s
day care home is indirectly affected by a governmental decision.
The standard discussed above (Regulation 18702.2)
would apply with
respect to clients which are business entities.
As to clients who are individuals,
which is more likely under
your facts,
Regulation l87026, provides the effect of a decision
is material if the decision will affect the individual’s income,
investments,
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property)
by $1,000 or more or the decision will
affect the individual’s real property interests in a matter that
is considered material under Regulation 18702.3 or Regulation
18702.4.
3.
Personal Effect
Finally, Regulation 18702.1(a) (4)
provides that the effect of
a decision is material if the decision will result in the personal
expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities of the official or his or
her immediate family increasing or decreasing by $250.
Thus,
even where there may be no indirect material financial effect on
an official’s economic interests, disqualification may be required
where Regulation 18702.1(a) (4) applies.


Our File No. A-94—272
Page 5
Under your facts, it does not appear that the decision would
have a material financial effect on you personally.
Moreover,
the.decision also will not have a financial impact on your
spouse’s business or its clients.
If this is the case, you may
participate in decisions pertaining to the special use permit in
question.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59Ol.
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1O per page.
